attributes.stem:
A 27-year-old woman presents to the postnatal ward review three days after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery of her first baby. She sustained a first-degree perineal tear during delivery. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears tearful but well-kempt with vital signs within normal limits. She is engaging appropriately with her baby and successfully breastfeeding. She reports feeling overwhelmed and anxious about caring for her baby at home. She describes disrupted sleep and meals due to feeding demands. She denies thoughts of self-harm or harm to others, and there are no features of psychosis or trauma response.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of baby blues, which affects up to 80% of new mothers. The timing (day 3), preserved functioning, and nature of symptoms (tearfulness, feeling overwhelmed) are characteristic. Despite these symptoms, she maintains good engagement with her baby and healthcare providers.

Baby blues typically develops around day 3-4 postpartum and resolves within 10 days. It is characterised by tearfulness, anxiety, and feeling overwhelmed, but importantly does not significantly impact function or bonding with the baby. No specific treatment is required beyond support and reassurance.

Perinatal depression requires symptoms to be present for at least two weeks and causes significant functional impairment. Key features include persistent low mood or anhedonia, along with changes in sleep, appetite, energy, and concentration. This patient's presentation is too early and too mild to meet these criteria. See Table below for differences between baby blues and perinatal depression (Image).

Perinatal anxiety disorder involves excessive, persistent worry along with physical symptoms such as panic attacks, restlessness, and difficulty concentrating. While this patient expresses some anxiety about going home, this is a normal concern for new parents and does not reach the threshold for a disorder.

Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency characterised by delusions, hallucinations, severe mood disturbance, and disorganised behaviour. There are no features of psychosis in this presentation.

Post-traumatic stress disorder in the perinatal period typically involves intrusive memories, flashbacks, avoidance behaviours, and hypervigilance related to a traumatic birth experience. This patient has had an uncomplicated delivery and shows no trauma symptoms.
learningPoints: Baby blues affects up to 80% of new mothers, typically peaks around day 3-4, and resolves within 10 days postpartum
Baby blues is characterised by tearfulness, anxiety, and feeling overwhelmed but with preserved function and bonding
Perinatal depression requires symptoms to be present for at least two weeks and causes significant functional impairment
references: Ali E. Women's experiences with postpartum anxiety disorders: a narrative literature review. Int J Womens Health. 2018;10:237-249. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29881312/
Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder. In: National Institute of mental health [online. 2019. Available at: https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/post-traumatic-stress-disorder-ptsd.
Postpartum psychosis. In: Royal College of Psychiatrists [online]. 2018. Available at: https://www.rcpsych.ac.uk/mental-health/problems-disorders/postpartum-psychosis.
Langan R, Goodbred AJ. Identification and Management of Peripartum Depression. Am Fam Physician. 2016 May 15;93(10):852-8. Available at: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2016/0515/p852.html/1000
